antelope.fun_fact: Antelopes can run up to 60 mph and are among the fastest land animals on Earth.

badger.fun_fact: Badgers can dig a new burrow in just one night using their powerful claws.

bat.fun_fact: Bats are the only mammals capable of true flight and navigate using echolocation.

bear.fun_fact: Bears have an extraordinary sense of smell — 7 times better than a bloodhound.

bee.fun_fact: A single honeybee will produce only 1/12 teaspoon of honey in its entire lifetime.

beetle.fun_fact: Beetles make up about 25% of all known animal species on Earth.

bison.fun_fact: American bison can run at speeds of 40 mph and jump fences over 5 feet tall.

boar.fun_fact: Wild boars have an incredibly tough snout they use to dig up roots, tubers, and truffles.

butterfly.fun_fact: Butterflies taste with their feet — they have taste sensors on their tarsi.

cat.fun_fact: Cats can make over 100 vocal sounds, whereas dogs can only make about 10.

caterpillar.fun_fact: A caterpillar's body completely dissolves into a nutrient soup inside its chrysalis before becoming a butterfly.

chimpanzee.fun_fact: Chimpanzees share about 98.7% of their DNA with humans and can use tools.

cockroach.fun_fact: Cockroaches can survive without their head for up to a week.

cow.fun_fact: Cows have panoramic vision covering 300° and can see almost all the way around them.

coyote.fun_fact: Coyotes have expanded their range across North America due to their adaptability.

crab.fun_fact: Crabs communicate by drumming or waving their claws and can regenerate lost limbs.

crow.fun_fact: Crows are one of the few animals that make their own tools and can recognize human faces.

deer.fun_fact: Deer antlers are the fastest-growing tissue in the animal kingdom, growing up to an inch per day.

dog.fun_fact: Dogs can smell 10,000–100,000 times better than humans and have 300 million olfactory receptors.

dolphin.fun_fact: Dolphins sleep with one eye open, resting half their brain at a time — called unihemispheric sleep.

donkey.fun_fact: Donkeys have an incredible memory and can remember places and other donkeys from 25 years ago.

dragonfly.fun_fact: Dragonflies are the most successful hunters in the animal kingdom, catching 95% of their prey attempts.

duck.fun_fact: Ducks have waterproof feathers due to an oil they spread with their beaks from a gland near their tail.

eagle.fun_fact: Eagles can spot prey from 2 miles away thanks to eyes with 5 times more light-sensing cells than humans.

elephant.fun_fact: Elephants are the only animals that can't jump, but they can communicate via infrasound across miles.

flamingo.fun_fact: Flamingos are pink because of the carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

fly.fun_fact: Flies taste food with their feet and process visual information 7 times faster than humans.

fox.fun_fact: Foxes use Earth's magnetic field as a rangefinder to gauge distance when pouncing on prey.

goat.fun_fact: Goats have rectangular pupils that give them a 320° field of view to spot predators.

goldfish.fun_fact: Goldfish have a memory span of at least 3 months, disproving the 3-second myth.

goose.fun_fact: Geese fly in V-formation which increases their flight range by 71% through aerodynamic uplift.

gorilla.fun_fact: Gorillas share 98.3% of their DNA with humans and can learn sign language.

grasshopper.fun_fact: Grasshoppers have ears on their bellies and can jump 20 times their body length.

hamster.fun_fact: Hamsters can carry half their body weight in food pouches in their cheeks.

hare.fun_fact: Hares are born fully furred with open eyes, unlike rabbits which are born blind and hairless.

hedgehog.fun_fact: Hedgehogs are immune to snake venom and can consume venomous snakes without harm.

hippopotamus.fun_fact: Hippos secrete a natural sunscreen called blood sweat that also acts as an antibiotic.

hornbill.fun_fact: Female hornbills seal themselves inside a tree cavity to incubate eggs, fed by the male through a slit.

horse.fun_fact: Horses can sleep both standing up and lying down and have the largest eyes of any land mammal.

hummingbird.fun_fact: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards and beat their wings up to 80 times per second.

hyena.fun_fact: Spotted hyenas have a stronger bite force relative to body size than lions.

jellyfish.fun_fact: The immortal jellyfish (Turritopsis dohrnii) can revert to its juvenile form and is considered biologically immortal.

kangaroo.fun_fact: Kangaroos cannot walk backwards and are the only large animal that uses hopping as primary locomotion.

koala.fun_fact: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to humans — so similar they can confuse crime scene investigators.

ladybugs.fun_fact: Ladybugs can play dead and secrete a foul-tasting fluid from their leg joints to deter predators.

leopard.fun_fact: Leopards are strong enough to carry prey twice their own body weight up into trees.

lion.fun_fact: A lion's roar can be heard from 5 miles away and is used to communicate territory and group location.

lizard.fun_fact: Some lizards can detach their tails as a distraction for predators and regrow them later.

lobster.fun_fact: Lobsters are biologically immortal — they don't weaken with age and just keep growing indefinitely.

mosquito.fun_fact: Female mosquitoes can lay up to 300 eggs at a time and need blood only for egg production.

moth.fun_fact: Moths navigate by the moon but are confused by artificial lights, causing their distinctive circling behavior.

mouse.fun_fact: Mice are capable of singing complex ultrasonic songs to communicate and attract mates.

octopus.fun_fact: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and can change color despite being colorblind.

okapi.fun_fact: The okapi was unknown to science until 1901 and is the only living relative of the giraffe.

orangutan.fun_fact: Orangutans share 96.9% of human DNA and are the only great apes that are largely solitary.

otter.fun_fact: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart — called a 'raft'.

owl.fun_fact: Owls can rotate their heads up to 270° because they have 14 cervical vertebrae (humans have 7).

ox.fun_fact: Oxen are castrated bulls specifically trained for draft work and can pull loads heavier than their body weight.

oyster.fun_fact: Oysters can change their sex multiple times throughout their lives depending on environmental conditions.

panda.fun_fact: Giant pandas have a false thumb — an elongated wrist bone — to help them grip bamboo.

parrot.fun_fact: African grey parrots can understand and use language contextually at the level of a 5-year-old child.

pelecaniformes.fun_fact: Pelicans have a throat pouch that can hold 3 gallons of water to scoop up fish.

penguin.fun_fact: Emperor penguins can dive to 1,850 feet and hold their breath for over 20 minutes.

pig.fun_fact: Pigs are among the most intelligent animals — smarter than dogs and comparable to 3-year-old children.

pigeon.fun_fact: Pigeons can recognize themselves in a mirror — one of very few animals with this level of self-awareness.

porcupine.fun_fact: Porcupine quills are coated with antibiotic fatty acids to protect the porcupine if it accidentally pricks itself.

possum.fun_fact: Possums 'play dead' involuntarily — it is an involuntary shock response, not a conscious act.

raccoon.fun_fact: Raccoons can remember solutions to tasks for up to 3 years and have nimble hands like primates.

rat.fun_fact: Rats laugh (in ultrasonic frequencies) when tickled and are highly empathetic to the suffering of other rats.

reindeer.fun_fact: Reindeer are the only deer species where both males and females grow antlers.

rhinoceros.fun_fact: Rhino horn is made of keratin — the same protein as human fingernails and hair.

sandpiper.fun_fact: The bar-tailed godwit (a sandpiper) holds the record for the longest non-stop flight: 7,500 miles in 11 days.

seahorse.fun_fact: Seahorses are the only species where the male carries and gives birth to young.

seal.fun_fact: Seals can hold their breath for up to 2 hours and dive to depths of 1,000 meters.

shark.fun_fact: Sharks are older than trees — they've existed for over 450 million years on Earth.

sheep.fun_fact: Sheep can recognize up to 50 other sheep faces and remember them for 2 years.

snake.fun_fact: Snakes smell with their tongue, flicking it to collect scent particles from the air.

sparrow.fun_fact: House sparrows were introduced to North America in 1851 and now number over 500 million worldwide.

squid.fun_fact: Squids can jet-propel at 25 mph and have the largest eyes of any living animal relative to body size.

squirrel.fun_fact: Squirrels plant thousands of trees annually by forgetting where they buried their acorns.

starfish.fun_fact: Starfish have no brain or blood — they use seawater instead of blood to pump nutrients around their body.

swan.fun_fact: Swans mate for life and can live for over 20 years, returning to the same nesting site each year.

tiger.fun_fact: Tigers are the only striped big cat — their stripes are unique like fingerprints and extend to their skin.

turkey.fun_fact: Wild turkeys can fly at up to 55 mph over short distances, despite their domestic relatives being flightless.

turtle.fun_fact: Sea turtles navigate using Earth's magnetic field and return to the exact beach where they were born to nest.

whale.fun_fact: Blue whales are the largest animals ever known to exist, with hearts the size of a small car.

wolf.fun_fact: Wolf packs don't follow the 'alpha' model — they are actually cooperative family units led by parents.

wombat.fun_fact: Wombats produce cube-shaped droppings — the only animal known to do so — used to mark territory.

woodpecker.fun_fact: Woodpeckers have a specially adapted skull with shock-absorbing spongy bone to prevent brain damage.

zebra.fun_fact: Each zebra's stripe pattern is unique as a fingerprint, and stripes may confuse predators by distorting speed perception.
